body:
---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: GEN ADAM NELSON <@bonako.com>
Date: Mon, Jan 16, 2023 at 5:49 AM
Subject: I am contacting you for the second time.
To:

Greeting,

I am General Adam Nelson, one of the Commanding generals in the U.S Army
and presently serving in Syria.

Please I urgently need you to help me safeguard an amount of money I have
here in my possession which is worth the sum of Nineteen million US
Dollars ($19 Million).

I came across this mega cash during an operation. You may have heard about
one of the heaviest attack we had that nuetralized hundreds of ISIS
terrorists in the past few months and it was during this operation that we
discovered this huge amount which we later distributed among the top
officers present during the operations. Considering how horrible and risky
it is here in this military camp, I deemed it necessary to look out for a
trusted fellow who would assist me in safeguarding the cash until I get
out of this horrible zone. It was to this effect that I started searching
online for an honest person whom I can trust to help me safeguard the cash
and I came across your mail address and I want to know if you are willing
to help me do this.

I want you to know that I am willing to offer you 35%-Percent of the total
amount if you do help me safeguard this money and get it out of here, I
will email you the details on how I plan on moving the cash out of this
place as soon as I receive your response.

Please get back to me ASAP

Thank you
Gen. Adam Nelson

Sofortiges Handeln erforderlich! Ihre Geräte sind ungeschützt und gefährdet...

Rabatt:
95.99% OFF

subject: Support Performance Troubleshooting
body: During peak hours, users encounter substantial performance lag attributed to heightened server load. Efforts to optimize database queries and scale server capacity have been unfruitful. Assistance is needed to resolve this issue.

subject: Re: [Python-Dev] Python-Dev Summary Draft (April 1-15, 2007)
body: Calvin Spealman wrote: > I have not gotten any replies about this. No comments, suggestions for > not skipping any missed threads, or corrections. Is everyone good with > this or should I give it another day or two? Part of the issue, for me anyway, is that many of the really "interesting" conversations have moved to Python-3000 and Python-ideas. That being said: There are a few threads in the "skipped" section that I would have liked to understand better, without having to read through all those messages, such as the various "decimal" threads. Other than that, the summaries remain very valuable. Thank you :) -- Talin _______________________________________________ Python-Dev mailing list zvllln-eum@python.org http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/python-dev Unsubscribe: http://mail.python.org/mailman/options/python-dev/python-dev%40tangomu.com

subject: Brexit blow
body: Plus, US debt ceiling negotiations, and accusations of ‘industrial-scale corruption’ in Teesside ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ All newsletters | Read in browser Patrick Jenkins Deputy Editor ‌ ‌ May 18 2023 Carmakers are calling on the UK government to renegotiate the terms of its Brexit trade deal to avoid a major blow to Britain’s car industry. On Monday, Stellantis — owner of Vauxhall and Peugeot — warned the UK parliament that Britain’s trade rules with the EU could force the closure of its Ellesmere Port factory by exposing electric vehicles to steep tariffs. Stellantis’s warning has revived a row about “rules of origin”, which penalise carmakers for using parts made outside the UK or EU. With those markets lagging behind Asia in EV battery production, Stellantis argued the rules would make their business “uncompetitive and unsustainable”. This excellent FT explainer sets out what is at stake.EU carmakers are joining British companies in lobbying for a change to the rules © FT montage/Bloomberg My choices this week ‌ ‌ US Treasury secretary Janet Yellen has warned that the country could run out of cash by June 1 if Congress does not raise the federal debt limit. The FT’s chief economics commentator Martin Wolf unpacks the political dynamics putting the US at risk of economic and financial catastrophe.(If you’d like a free read of Martin’s views on the game of chicken US politicians are playing, please download our iPhone app, FT Edit, where it ran in yesterday’s US edition and will appear tomorrow everywhere else. You don’t need to register or give payment details, you can simply download and start reading FT Edit, and the first month is free.) Luxury private aviation company VistaJet has experienced rapid growth, despite a troubling combination of debt, net losses and short-term liabilities. Investigative reporter Dan McCrum digs into the debt-fuelled ascent of VistaJet — and the man directing its bold dealmaking. President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan defied expectations in Turkey’s general election last Sunday, winning 49.5 per cent of the vote and moving into pole position ahead of a final run-off at the end of the month. But how will western leaders respond to five more years of Erdoğan? (free to read)Three UK High Court judges invested in controversial tax avoidance schemes that have themselves been challenged in court. This FT investigation highlights important questions about the transparency — or lack thereof — when it comes to the UK’s top judges and their financial interests. The regeneration of a Teesside steel plant — touted as one of the UK’s biggest levelling-up projects — has been thrown into the spotlight with Tees Valley mayor Ben Houchen facing accusations of “industrial-scale corruption”. Our northern England correspondent Jennifer Williams breaks down the deals made behind closed doors. (free to read)Who is a digital native now? Columnist Stephen Bush draws on wisdom from Grampa Abe Simpson as he reflects on endless — hopeless — efforts to stay technologically savvy, and what it means for tackling knotty policy questions. Thanks for reading,PatrickPS Don’t forget our special subscription offer, which means you get a third off a Standard subscription to the Financial Times. This also gives you access to our excellent newsletters, including the award-winning Inside Politics, and our flagship news briefing, FirstFT. ‌ ‌ Recommended newsletters for you In Today’s FT Your daily overview of FT’s top stories. subject: A harsh harsh truth
body:
You're receiving this email because you subscribed after being recommended by Techpresso. If you'd like to unsubscribe, you can click here.

Here’s a harsh truth that might change your life…

You must develop the ability to be disliked in order to free yourself from the prison of other people’s opinions.

Remember yesterday I told you about my newsletter you signed up for?

It’s called Your Next Breakthrough and every Monday, I’ll send you harsh, life-changing truths along with a question to ask yourself, and an exercise to try out.

So, here’s today’s question:

What are three things in your life that you are willing to be disliked for?

If you have trouble answering that question, that is a problem.

Tomorrow, I’ll share an exercise you can try out to help you further develop the ability to be disliked. And an inspiring story from one of our readers that may spark an interesting breakthrough for you.

If you no longer wish to receive this newsletter and all other updates, you can unsubscribe.
